name: The Tyranny of Numbers: Counting Calories, Counting Growth
description:
What constitutes "good statistics"?

This lecture explores how some of the most familiar indicators: calories often used to depict the quality of our diet and Gross Domestic Product, which does the same for the economy, illustrate both the power and the limits of measurement. Calories provide a useful estimate of energy intake, but they do not fully capture nutritional quality, health outcomes, or the lived experience of eating. In a similar way, GDP measures economic output, but not inequality, environmental stress, unpaid care work, or the broader quality of life. In both cases, a single statistic can illuminate one dimension of reality while obscuring others.

Through this discussion TCA Anant examines why modern institutions are so drawn to numerical indicators. Governments need targets, firms need metrics, and citizens seek simple benchmarks in an increasingly complex world. But when measures become goals in themselves, distortions often follow: food engineered for labels rather than nourishment, education reduced to test scores, or growth pursued without regard to employment, sustainability, or social well-being.

name: Wild And Free: Biodiversity On Our Food Plates
description:
How biodiverse are our plates?

In this talk Rajkamal Goswami explores how human food systems that were once entirely dependent on hunting, foraging, and gathering from the wild still retain traces of those ancient relationships, even within the most urbanised corners of the world. Drawing from over eight years of research across northeast India, the talk journeys through the remarkable diversity of wild foods consumed by different cultures, and the deep ecological knowledge, skill, labour, and adventure that keep these connections alive. 

It will also seek to explore important questions like : how are these food systems being reshaped by climate change, land-use transformation, markets, and rapid technological and cultural shifts? Are these changes always destructive, or do they also create new possibilities for adaptation? What do we lose when wild foods disappear from our diets and how are communities innovating to ensure that the wilderness continues to survive on our plates?

name: Food as Methodology
description:
What happens when food is used as a method, not just a medium? Can we draw artistic, social, and political insight from food?

Join artist Barney Pau for a deep dive into his exhibit ‘Fortify,’ a film on the fortification of flour that evokes the horror of contemporary food production practices. He will introduce his art practice and how he uses food as both a source of inspiration and a medium. He will explore how queer theory and ecology interact and shape his work, and how he uses the processes of foraging and fermentation as artistic methodologies to unpack complex social, political, and environmental matter.

name: The Calorie Footprint: From Plate to Planet
description:
What if every calorie we consume tells a story about a changing planet?
This walkthrough traces the hidden journey of food beyond our plates, from the soil where crops are grown to the systems that transport them across cities and countries. As we move through the exhibits, we will explore how food systems and regulations shape ecosystems, communities, and the environment. In the proposed Anthropocene era, human activity and the global demand for food are linked to rising temperatures, changing landscapes, and collapsing biodiversity. Through shared reflections and simple questions, this experience invites visitors to consider how everyday consumption is deeply connected to planetary change.

name: CALORIE meets Ghibli: People, Food and even more Stories
description:
Studio Ghibli, the Japanese animation studio known for stories with heart and meaning, teams up with CALORIE once more, for a second serving of food for thought.

The studio has a recurring theme of cooking and eating to connect the viewers and the worlds within the Ghibli movies. And when looking at an exhibition which uses food as a common thread, the two coming together was inevitable. 

We will be looking into themes of history, politics and the environment, exploring new connections between the exhibits and our favourite movies. 

So, are you ready to be Spirited Away? Will you Totoro-ly love it? Where might this Moving Castle take us now?

If you got these puns…this walkthrough will be right up your alley!
And if not? Come find out!

name: CALORIE meets Ghibli: Gab and Gobble
description:
Have you ever wanted to step into a Ghibli movie? Been keen on exploring the mystical landscapes, meet the whimsical characters or taste the mouth-watering dishes you saw on screen? 

CALORIE Meets Ghibli: Gab and Gobble is your canvas to do so, bringing together a studio that builds connection through the acts of cooking and eating, and an exhibition about humanity and food.

Experimentors Kruthika and Vinaya invite you to join them for a Ghibli-ful day at Science Gallery Bengaluru, for good conversation, insightful exploration and a warm meal. 

Through this programme you get a chance to transform the gallery space into one for reflection, fun and imagination, connecting Ghibli’s tales to Calorie’s themes.

name: Munch on This!
description:
What happens when an exhibition follows you onto the plate?

Munch on This! is a walkthrough and a meal where the gallery slowly turns into the menu. Moving through selected exhibits from CALORIE, you'll encounter ingredients, food histories, and ideas that may already live in your kitchen. Then you'll sit down and eat some of them.

Expect small bites, shared dishes, and mealtime banter. The food takes cues from the exhibition but we're not giving the menu away just yet. Half the fun is in the finding out!

Come at least slightly open to surprises and willing to let lunch ask you a few questions. Join our Experimentors, Sai and Nirupama, in this edible side quest.

Seconds are encouraged! Leaving hungry is not an option.

name: Eat, Read, Think
description:
Eat, Read, Think is a reading circle run by Science Gallery Bengaluru, bringing together curious minds to explore ideas through literature and conversation. Readings will be themed around the current exhibition season, CALORIE, exploring humanity's fraught and intimate relationship with food through readings inspired by the many exhibits present throughout the gallery. Eat, Read, Think welcomes young people from all walks of life to gather for conversations that connect science, culture and society.

The eighth edition of the circle will read excerpts from 'Soda Politics: Taking on Big Soda (and Winning)' by Marion Nestle.

Come for community stay for snacks!

name: Fortify: Flour, Futures & Fermentation
description:
What does flour carry beyond nutrition? 

Histories of agriculture, changing ecologies, microbial life, labour, and questions of resilience. FORTIFY: Flour, Futures & Fermentation is a participatory workshop that uses Barney Pau’s film FORTIFY as a starting point to explore wheat not simply as a staple ingredient, but as a political, ecological, and cultural material.

Bringing together farmer perspectives, sensory engagement, conversation, and baking, the workshop traces the journey of grain—from seed to flour to fermentation—through the lens of biodiversity and food systems. Participants will engage with questions around the Green Revolution, heritage grains, milling practices, microbial life, and contemporary baking, while exploring how texture, flavour, nutrition, and fermentation are shaped by agricultural diversity.

Set within the Indian context, the session features perspectives from across the grain ecosystem: heritage grain cultivation with Anushka Neyol (Three-One Farms), flour transformation and baking with Pranav Ullal (Loafer & Co.), and circular food systems and by-products with Elizabeth Yorke (Edible Issues/Saving Grains). Through film, dialogue, and hands-on engagement, the workshop invites participants to rethink flour as a living material embedded within larger ecological and cultural futures.

name: Blind Biscuit Taste Test: Deconstructing the Biscuit
description:
Blind Biscuit Taste Test: Deconstructing the Biscuit explores how flavour is shaped not only by ingredients, but by processing, memory, expectation, and sensory perception. Through a blind tasting of anonymised biscuit samples, participants are invited to question what they think they are tasting—and why.

The session begins with a conversation on the science and systems behind biscuits: from flour, fats, flavourings, and enriching agents to questions of nutrition, agriculture, and food processing. Participants then move into a guided tasting, mapping flavours, textures, perceived ingredients, and personal associations across a series of crushed biscuit samples.

Together, the group reflects on the gaps between perception and reality before co-creating a collective Biscuit Flavour Wheel—a sensory map of tastes, aromas, textures, aftertastes, and interpretations. The workshop closes by asking: What is “real” flavour, and how does processing shape the way we eat?

This participatory session blends food systems, sensory inquiry, and collective making to rethink one of the most familiar foods on our plate.

name: Bitter, Sweet, and Everything in Between: The Language of Food and How we talk about What we Eat
description:
This walkthrough explores the language we use around food, rather than food itself. Moving through the exhibition, we’ll notice the words and phrases that shape how food is described - from comfort food and junk food to clean eating and guilty pleasures.

Through close looking and playful prompts,join our Experimentor Shreya for a walkthrough that opens up space, to notice and to explore the many ways food is spoken about - how it is named, framed, and talked about across different contexts, and what these words might quietly suggest or leave unsaid in everyday life.
